findings.title: Possible SSH brute-force attempt
findings.remediation: Block the source IP, enforce key-based auth, and deploy fail2ban.
findings.rationale: 42 'Failed password' lines originate from 203.0.113.10, at or above the configured threshold of 5 — a volume characteristic of credential guessing rather than user error.

findings.title: Publicly accessible S3 bucket
findings.description: S3 bucket 'prod-backups' grants access to a public group (AllUsers).
findings.remediation: Remove public ACL grants and enable S3 Block Public Access.
findings.rationale: The bucket ACL grants to AllUsers; that group resolves to anyone on the internet, so the bucket is public.

findings.title: Security group open to the world on SSH
findings.description: Security group 'sg-0a1b2c' allows 0.0.0.0/0 inbound on port 22 (SSH).
findings.remediation: Restrict inbound SSH (port 22) to known IP ranges instead of 0.0.0.0/0.
findings.rationale: An inbound rule permits 0.0.0.0/0 (the whole internet) to reach port 22, which exposes SSH — a remote administration service — to untrusted networks.

findings.title: Possible port scan
findings.remediation: Investigate the source host; block it if unauthorized.
findings.rationale: 198.51.100.23 touched 27 distinct destination ports, at or above the threshold of 10. Fanning out across many ports on a host is characteristic of service discovery, not normal traffic.
findings.verify: Review the flow log / capture for connections from 198.51.100.23.

findings.title: IAM user without MFA
findings.description: IAM user 'deploy-bot' has no MFA device enabled.
findings.remediation: Enable an MFA device for this IAM user.
findings.rationale: ListMFADevices for 'deploy-bot' returned no devices, so the user's console/API access is protected by a password alone.

findings.title: Direct privileged login
findings.remediation: Disable direct root/admin SSH login; require sudo from named accounts.
findings.rationale: An 'Accepted password' line for the privileged account 'root' shows a direct privileged login, which bypasses per-user accountability that sudo from a named account provides.

findings.title: Missing security header: Content-Security-Policy
findings.description: The response from https://app.example.com does not set the Content-Security-Policy header.
findings.remediation: Add a Content-Security-Policy header to mitigate XSS.
findings.rationale: The response to GET https://app.example.com (HTTP 200) did not include a Content-Security-Policy header, so browsers fall back to unsafe defaults.

findings.title: Missing security header: X-Frame-Options
findings.description: The response from https://app.example.com does not set the X-Frame-Options header.
findings.rationale: The response to GET https://app.example.com (HTTP 200) did not include an X-Frame-Options header, so browsers fall back to unsafe defaults.
